md: A **quadrilateral** is any four-sided polygon. The angles of any quadrilateral sum to **360°** (you'll see why in the next subtopic on polygon angle sums). But there are five named special quadrilaterals, each with its own properties — and they form a hierarchy where some are special cases of others.
### The five families
**Trapezoid**: at least one pair of parallel sides.
**Parallelogram**: two pairs of parallel sides.
**Rectangle**: a parallelogram with four right angles.
**Rhombus**: a parallelogram with all four sides equal.
**Square**: a parallelogram that is *both* a rectangle AND a rhombus — four equal sides AND four right angles.

blocks.title: The hierarchy
blocks.body: Every **square** is also a rhombus, a rectangle, AND a parallelogram. Every **rectangle** and **rhombus** is also a parallelogram. Every **parallelogram** is also a trapezoid (under the inclusive definition that 'at least one pair of parallel sides' is enough). The shapes nest — narrower categories inherit all properties of broader ones.

md: ### Properties of parallelograms
If you have a parallelogram, all of these hold automatically:
**Opposite sides equal** (and parallel).
**Opposite angles equal**.
**Consecutive angles supplementary** (each pair on the same side of one diagonal sums to 180°).
**Diagonals bisect each other** (each diagonal cuts the other in half).
### Adding constraints
**Rectangle**: parallelogram + four right angles. Extra property: **diagonals are equal in length**.
**Rhombus**: parallelogram + all sides equal. Extra properties: **diagonals are perpendicular** and **bisect the angles** at each vertex.
**Square**: rectangle + rhombus. Has **all** the properties of both.
### Trapezoid specifics

The two parallel sides are called **bases**; the non-parallel sides are **legs**. An **isosceles trapezoid** is a trapezoid where the two legs are equal in length — its diagonals are also equal, and the base angles (the angles next to each base) are equal.

blocks.title: Diagnose a quadrilateral
blocks.body: Given a quadrilateral with unknown properties: (1) Are any sides parallel? Zero pairs → general quadrilateral. One pair → trapezoid. Two pairs → parallelogram. (2) If parallelogram: are the angles right? → rectangle. Are the sides all equal? → rhombus. Both? → square.

md: ### Worked example
A parallelogram has one angle measuring 70°. Find the other three angles.
Opposite angles are equal — so the angle opposite the 70° one is also **70°**. Consecutive angles are supplementary — the two angles next to the 70° one each measure $180 - 70 = $ **110°**.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Five families: **trapezoid** (1 pair parallel) ⊃ **parallelogram** (2 pairs parallel) ⊃ **rectangle** (right angles) and **rhombus** (equal sides). **Square** is both a rectangle and a rhombus. Diagonals tell you a lot: equal → rectangle; perpendicular → rhombus; both → square.
